---
title: "Appendix F - High-Performance Computing with Talapas"
author: "Bill Cresko"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: left
    code-fold: false
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
editor: visual
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false

library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)

theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 14))
set.seed(2026)
```

# Appendix F: High-Performance Computing with Talapas

## Overview

::: {.callout-tip title="Pre-requisite Material"}
This appendix covers the University of Oregon's high-performance computing (HPC) cluster, Talapas. You will learn how to access the cluster, submit jobs using SLURM, manage software modules, and run GPU-accelerated computations. See Appendix A for background on cluster computing concepts.
:::

# What is Talapas?

## Talapas Overview

-   **Talapas** is UO's high-performance computing (HPC) cluster
-   Named from Chinook word for coyote (educator and keeper of knowledge)
-   Managed by Research Advanced Computing Services (RACS)
-   Located in the Allen Hall Data Center
-   Documentation: [uoracs.github.io/talapas2-knowledge-base](https://uoracs.github.io/talapas2-knowledge-base/)

### Key Resources:

-   Over 14,520 CPU cores
-   129 TB of memory
-   89 GPUs (including A100, H100, V100)
-   3+ Petabytes of storage

## Talapas Architecture

![Talapas Architecture](images/appendix_F_S03_talapas_architecture.svg){fig-align="center" width="85%"}

<!--
DUPLICATE SLIDE - Original Mermaid (remove when SVG is finalized)

## Talapas Architecture (Mermaid Original)

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 10
%%| fig-height: 6
%%{init: {'theme': 'base', 'themeVariables': {'fontSize': '18px', 'fontFamily': 'arial', 'primaryTextColor': '#000000'}}}%%
flowchart TB
    U[Your Computer] --> SSH([SSH])
    SSH --> L1[login1]
    SSH --> L2[login2]
    SSH --> L3[login3]
    SSH --> L4[login4]
    L1 --> S[SLURM Scheduler]
    L2 --> S
    L3 --> S
    L4 --> S
    S --> SB([sbatch])
    S --> SR([srun])
    SB --> C1[CPU Nodes]
    SR --> C1
    SB --> C2[GPU Nodes]
    SB --> C3[Fat Nodes]
    C1 --> ST[(Storage)]
    C2 --> ST
    C3 --> ST

    style U fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style L1 fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style L2 fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style L3 fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style L4 fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style S fill:#f9d71c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style C1 fill:#98fb98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style C2 fill:#ffa07a,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style C3 fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style ST fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style SSH fill:#ffffff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style SB fill:#ffffff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style SR fill:#ffffff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```
-->

# Accessing Talapas

## Login Nodes vs Compute Nodes

::: panel-tabset
### Login Nodes

-   Where you land when you SSH into Talapas
-   For **light tasks only**: file management, editing, job submission
-   Shared by all users --- be considerate!
-   Heavy processes will be killed

### Compute Nodes

-   Where your actual work runs
-   Accessed through the SLURM scheduler
-   Different types for different needs:
    -   Standard CPU nodes
    -   GPU nodes
    -   High-memory ("fat") nodes
:::

## Node Types

| Node Type      | Cores   | Memory     | Purpose                |
|----------------|---------|------------|------------------------|
| Standard CPU   | 28--128 | 128--512 GB| General computation    |
| Large Memory   | 56      | 1--4 TB    | Memory-intensive work  |
| GPU (K80/A100) | 28--48  | 256--512 GB| Machine learning, CUDA |
| Interactive    | Various | Various    | Testing, development   |

### Partitions (Queues):

-   `compute` --- General purpose CPU partition (default)
-   `short` --- Jobs up to 24 hours
-   `long` --- Jobs up to 14 days
-   `gpu` / `longgpu` --- GPU-enabled nodes
-   `fat` / `longfat` --- High-memory nodes
-   `preempt` --- Access to all nodes (may be interrupted)

## Getting Access

::: panel-tabset
### Step 1: Join a PIRG

-   **PIRG** = Principal Investigator Research Group
-   Contact your PI to be added to their PIRG
-   Or contact RACS to create a new PIRG

### Step 2: Request Access

-   Visit [racs.uoregon.edu/request-access](https://racs.uoregon.edu/request-access)
-   Your username = your Duck ID
-   Your password = your UO password
:::

## Logging In

::: panel-tabset
### Web Browser

[ondemand.talapas.uoregon.edu](https://ondemand.talapas.uoregon.edu)

-   No command line needed!
-   File browser for uploads/downloads
-   Interactive applications: JupyterLab, RStudio, MATLAB, Remote desktop
-   Great for beginners and quick data analysis

### Command Line

``` bash
# Basic SSH connection
ssh yourduckid@login.talapas.uoregon.edu

# Or specific login node
ssh yourduckid@login1.talapas.uoregon.edu
```

**From Windows:** Use PuTTY, MobaXterm, or Windows Terminal

**Off Campus?** First connect to UO VPN, then SSH as normal
:::

# Storage on Talapas

## Storage Structure

::: panel-tabset
### Directory Map

![Storage Structure](images/appendix_F_S09_storage_structure.svg){fig-align="center" width="100%"}

<!--
DUPLICATE SLIDE - Original Mermaid (remove when SVG is finalized)

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 10
%%| fig-height: 4
%%{init: {'theme': 'base', 'themeVariables': {'fontSize': '18px', 'fontFamily': 'arial', 'primaryTextColor': '#000000'}}}%%
flowchart LR
    R["/"] --> H["/home"]
    R --> P["/projects"]
    R --> T["/tmp"]
    H --> HD["/home/duckid"]
    P --> PG["/projects/PIRG"]
    PG --> PGU["/projects/PIRG/duckid"]
    PG --> PGS["/projects/PIRG/shared"]

    style R fill:#ffffff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style H fill:#ffffff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style P fill:#ffffff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style PG fill:#ffffff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style HD fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style PGU fill:#98fb98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style PGS fill:#f9d71c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style T fill:#ffa07a,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```
-->

### Quotas

| Location                | Quota                 | Purpose                     |
|-------------------------|-----------------------|-----------------------------|
| `/home/duckid`          | 250 GB                | Config files, small scripts |
| `/projects/PIRG/duckid` | Shared (2 TB default) | Large data, analysis        |
| `/projects/PIRG/shared` | Shared                | Files shared with group     |
| `/tmp`                  | Local to node         | Temporary scratch space     |

### Important Notes

-   **Data is NOT automatically backed up!**
-   Use `/projects` for large files
-   Clean up when done

``` bash
# Check your storage usage
df -h ~
du -sh /projects/myPIRG/myusername
```
:::

# SLURM Job Scheduler

## What is SLURM?

-   **S**imple **L**inux **U**tility for **R**esource **M**anagement
-   Job scheduler and workload manager
-   Allocates resources fairly among users
-   Ensures efficient cluster utilization

![SLURM Job Workflow](images/appendix_F_S10_slurm_workflow.svg){fig-align="center" width="100%"}

<!--
DUPLICATE SLIDE - Original Mermaid (remove when SVG is finalized)

## What is SLURM? (Mermaid Original)

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 10
%%| fig-height: 4
%%{init: {'theme': 'base', 'themeVariables': {'fontSize': '18px', 'fontFamily': 'arial', 'primaryTextColor': '#000000'}}}%%
flowchart LR
    A[Submit Job] --> SB([sbatch])
    SB --> B[SLURM Queue]
    B --> W([Waits])
    W --> C[Job Runs on Compute Node]
    C --> CO([Completes])
    CO --> D[Output Files]

    style A fill:#f9d71c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style B fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style C fill:#98fb98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style D fill:#ffa07a,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style SB fill:#ffffff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style W fill:#ffffff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style CO fill:#ffffff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```
-->

### Key Concepts:

-   **Jobs**: Units of work you submit
-   **Partitions**: Queues with different properties
-   **Accounts**: Your PIRG for resource tracking

## Essential SLURM Commands

::: panel-tabset
### Submit & Monitor

``` bash
# Submit a batch job
sbatch myscript.sh

# Check your jobs in the queue
squeue -u $USER

# Check all jobs on a partition
squeue -p short

# Cancel a job
scancel JOBID

# Get detailed job info
scontrol show job JOBID
```

### After Completion

``` bash
# Check job history and resource usage
sacct -j JOBID --format=JobID,Elapsed,MaxRSS

# View partition information
sinfo -p short
```

### Status Codes

| Code | Meaning           |
|------|-------------------|
| PD   | Pending (waiting) |
| R    | Running           |
| CG   | Completing        |
| CD   | Completed         |
| F    | Failed            |
:::

## Interactive Jobs

::: panel-tabset
### Command

``` bash
# Start an interactive session
srun --pty --partition=compute --account=myPIRG \
     --time=2:00:00 --cpus-per-task=4 --mem=8G /bin/bash
```

### When to Use

-   Testing your workflow before batch submission
-   Debugging code
-   Quick data exploration
-   Running GUI applications

**Note**: Your prompt will change from `login1` to something like `n049`
:::

# Writing Batch Scripts

## SLURM Batch Script Template

::: panel-tabset
### Template

``` bash
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=my_analysis      # Job name
#SBATCH --partition=compute         # Partition (queue)
#SBATCH --account=myPIRG            # Your PIRG name
#SBATCH --output=output_%j.log      # Standard output (%j = job ID)
#SBATCH --error=error_%j.log        # Standard error
#SBATCH --time=04:00:00             # Time limit (HH:MM:SS)
#SBATCH --nodes=1                   # Number of nodes
#SBATCH --ntasks-per-node=1         # Tasks per node
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=4           # CPUs per task
#SBATCH --mem=16G                   # Memory per node

# Load required modules
module load R/4.3.3

# Run your program
Rscript my_analysis.R
```

### Directives

| Directive         | Description         | Example                           |
|-------------------|---------------------|-----------------------------------|
| `--partition`     | Queue to use        | `compute`, `short`, `long`, `gpu` |
| `--account`       | PIRG for accounting | `nereus`                          |
| `--time`          | Max runtime         | `1-12:00:00` (1 day, 12 hrs)      |
| `--nodes`         | Number of nodes     | `1`                               |
| `--cpus-per-task` | Cores per task      | `8`                               |
| `--mem`           | Memory per node     | `32G`                             |
| `--gres`          | Generic resources   | `gpu:1`                           |

### Interpretation

-   Lines starting with `#SBATCH` are directives for the scheduler
-   `%j` in filenames is replaced by the job ID
-   Request only what you need --- smaller jobs run sooner!
-   Always load modules after the SBATCH directives
:::

## GPU Jobs

::: panel-tabset
### Script

``` bash
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=gpu_job
#SBATCH --partition=gpu             # GPU partition
#SBATCH --account=myPIRG
#SBATCH --time=1-00:00:00           # 1 day
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --ntasks-per-node=1
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:1                # Request 1 GPU
#SBATCH --mem=32G

# Load CUDA and your software
module load cuda/12.4
module load miniconda3/20240410

# Activate your conda environment
conda activate my_ml_env

# Run your GPU code
python train_model.py
```

### Check GPUs

``` bash
# See available GPUs
/packages/racs/bin/slurm-show-gpus
```
:::

# Software Modules

## Managing Software with LMOD

::: panel-tabset
### Commands

``` bash
# List all available modules
module avail

# Search for specific software
module spider R
module spider python

# Load a module
module load R/4.3.3
module load miniconda3/20240410

# See what's loaded
module list

# Unload a module
module unload R

# Reset to default
module purge
```

### Best Practices

::: {.callout-note title="Module Tips"}
-   In batch scripts, always load modules after the SBATCH directives
-   Use `module spider` to find available versions
-   `module purge` resets your environment to defaults
-   Specify version numbers for reproducibility (e.g., `R/4.3.3` not just `R`)
:::
:::

# Best Practices

## Talapas Best Practices

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Do:

-   Request only resources you need
-   Test with small datasets first
-   Use `--time` conservatively
-   Clean up old files regularly
-   Use `/projects` for large data
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Don't:

-   Run heavy computation on login nodes
-   Leave jobs running indefinitely
-   Store sensitive data without encryption
-   Forget to acknowledge RACS in publications
:::
:::::

## Getting Help

### Resources:

-   **Documentation**: [uoracs.github.io/talapas2-knowledge-base](https://uoracs.github.io/talapas2-knowledge-base/)
-   **Service Desk**: [hpcrcf.atlassian.net/servicedesk](https://hpcrcf.atlassian.net/servicedesk/customer/portal/1)
-   **Email**: racs\@uoregon.edu
-   **Office Hours**: Knight Library, Dream Lab 122E (Wednesdays 1--3pm)

### Acknowledgment Text:

> "The authors acknowledge Research Advanced Computing Services (RACS) at the University of Oregon for providing computing resources that have contributed to the research results reported within this publication."
